esql_coding_standards.overview: Comprehensive ESQL coding standards for IBM App Connect Enterprise v13, including best practices, schema standards, variable management, and HTTP query parameter handling. These standards are derived from real-world enterprise implementations and IBM recommendations.

principle.name: Use REFERENCE for Tree Navigation
principle.description: Always use REFERENCE variables when navigating the message tree to avoid expensive tree copying operations
principle.rationale: REFERENCE variables point to tree locations without copying data, significantly improving performance especially with large messages

principle.name: Minimize Tree Copying
principle.description: Avoid unnecessary copying of the entire message tree

techniques.technique: Use CopyMessageHeaders() instead of CopyEntireMessage() when possible
Set specific fields rather than copying entire structures
Use CREATE FIELD for building new structures

principle.name: Proper Variable Scope and Declaration
principle.description: Declare variables at the appropriate scope with meaningful names

guidelines.guideline: Declare local variables at function/procedure level
Use descriptive names following camelCase convention

principle.name: Efficient Loop Processing
principle.description: Optimize loops to avoid performance bottlenecks

best_practices.practice: Do NOT use CARDINALITY inside loops
Calculate CARDINALITY once before the loop

principle.name: String Manipulation Efficiency
principle.description: Minimize use of string manipulation functions in performance-critical code

techniques.technique: Avoid repeated string concatenation in loops

principle.name: Field Existence Checking
principle.description: Use efficient methods to check if fields exist

principle.name: BROKER SCHEMA Declaration
principle.description: Every ESQL file MUST start with a BROKER SCHEMA declaration that defines the namespace for all modules, functions, and procedures in that file.

best_practices.practice: Match the package structure of your project
Use consistent schema across related ESQL files
For generated files, use 'gen' as part of schema: gen or com.organization.gen

example.code:
BROKER SCHEMA com.endur.esb.common
CREATE PROCEDURE BuildAudit( IN InputRoot REFERENCE, IN InputBody REFERENCE, IN Environment REFERENCE, IN InputLocalEnvironment REFERENCE ) BEGIN -- Procedure implementation END;

principle.name: Schema Consistency Within Project
principle.description: All ESQL files within the same project directory should use the same BROKER SCHEMA. The ESQL files should be placed in the directory that the namespace refers to. So if the schema is called com.weather.api, all esql files that use that schema must be placed in WeatherAPI_App/com/weather/api
principle.rationale: Ensures modules can reference each other without namespace conflicts
principle.example:
Project: WeatherAPI_App Directory: WeatherAPI_App/com/weather/api
All ESQL files should use: BROKER SCHEMA com.weather.api
Files: - ValidateInput.esql (BROKER SCHEMA com.weather.api) - PrepareCurrentWeatherRequest.esql (BROKER SCHEMA com.weather.api) - PrepareAlertsRequest.esql (BROKER SCHEMA com.weather.api) - HandleError.esql (BROKER SCHEMA com.weather.api)

principle.name: Generated vs Manual Code Schemas
principle.description: Distinguish between generated and manually written code using schema naming

guidelines.guideline: Generated code (from WSDL, etc.): Use 'gen' schema
Manual application code: Use application-specific schema

directory_structure.structure: ProjectName_App/ ├── gen/ # Generated files │ ├── Service.msgflow # Uses gen schema │ ├── Service_Compute.esql # BROKER SCHEMA gen │ └── SubFlow.subflow # Uses gen schema ├── com/organization/esb/ # Manual files │ ├── CustomFlow.msgflow # Uses com.organization.esb schema │ └── CustomLogic.esql # BROKER SCHEMA com.organization.esb

principle.name: Compute Node ESQL Reference Format
principle.description: Compute nodes must reference ESQL modules using the correct format that includes the schema namespace

components.component: The BROKER SCHEMA from the ESQL file

principle.name: Subflow References in Message Flows
principle.description: Subflows are referenced using their namespace path and file name

critical_rule.name: Single Declaration of EXTERNAL Variables
critical_rule.description: EXTERNAL variables MUST be declared only ONCE per project to avoid duplicate declaration errors during compilation and deployment.
critical_rule.rationale: When the same EXTERNAL variable is declared in multiple ESQL files within the same project, ACE will throw a duplicate declaration error during BAR file creation or deployment, causing the build to fail.
critical_rule.solution: Create a dedicated CommonDeclarations.esql file in your project to hold all EXTERNAL and SHARED variable declarations that need to be accessed across multiple modules.

pattern.name: common_declarations_file
pattern.description: Create a single ESQL file for all shared EXTERNAL and SHARED variable declarations

file_structure.purpose: Central location for all EXTERNAL and SHARED variable declarations

pattern.name: using_external_variables
pattern.description: How to use EXTERNAL variables declared in CommonDeclarations.esql
pattern.important_note: You do NOT need to redeclare EXTERNAL variables in other ESQL files. Once declared in CommonDeclarations.esql, they are automatically available to all modules within the same BROKER SCHEMA.

rule.name: EXTERNAL Variable Scope
rule.description: EXTERNAL variables are accessible to all modules within the same BROKER SCHEMA once declared in any ESQL file in the project
rule.scope: Project-wide within the same BROKER SCHEMA

rule.name: SHARED Variable Scope
rule.description: SHARED variables maintain state across message flow invocations and are accessible to all modules within the same BROKER SCHEMA
rule.scope: Project-wide within the same BROKER SCHEMA, persists across invocations

rule.name: Local Variable Scope
rule.description: Local variables (DECLARE without EXTERNAL or SHARED) are scoped to the function or procedure where they are declared
rule.declaration_requirement: Declare within each function/procedure as needed

recommended_structure.description: Organize ESQL files to clearly separate common declarations from module-specific logic
recommended_structure.structure: ProjectName_App/ ├── com/organization/project/ │ ├── CommonDeclarations.esql # All EXTERNAL and SHARED variables │ ├── Module1_Compute.esql # Module-specific logic (no EXTERNAL declarations) │ ├── Module2_Compute.esql # Module-specific logic (no EXTERNAL declarations) │ ├── Module3_Compute.esql # Module-specific logic (no EXTERNAL declarations) │ └── UtilityFunctions.esql # Common utility functions

convention.name: CommonDeclarations.esql
convention.description: Standard name for the file containing shared variable declarations

critical_rule.name: Accessing Query Parameters from HTTP Input
critical_rule.description: Query parameters from HTTP GET requests are accessible through InputLocalEnvironment.HTTP.Input.QueryString in ACE message flows when the HTTP Input node has parseQueryString="true" configured. Each query parameter becomes a field in the QueryString tree.

correct_approach.prerequisite: HTTP Input node must have parseQueryString="true" attribute set in the message flow

alternative_approach.code: -- Alternative approach (may work in some ACE versions) SET location = InputRoot.HTTPInputHeader.location;
alternative_approach.note: This approach may not work consistently across all ACE versions. The recommended approach is to use InputLocalEnvironment.HTTP.Input.QueryString with parseQueryString="true" configured on the HTTP Input node.

pattern.name: query_parameter_validation
pattern.description: Standard pattern for validating and extracting query parameters

pattern.name: multiple_query_parameters
url_encoded_parameters
pattern.description: Handling URL-encoded query parameters (spaces, special characters)
pattern.note: ACE automatically decodes URL-encoded parameters, so "New%20York" becomes "New York"

http_input_node_configuration.description: HTTP Input node configuration for proper query parameter handling

setting.name: URL Specifier
setting.description: The base path for the endpoint

setting.name: parseQueryString
setting.description: CRITICAL: Must be set to true to enable query parameter parsing
setting.how_to_set: In the message flow XML, add parseQueryString="true" attribute to the HTTP Input node:

setting.name: Message Domain
setting.description: Set based on expected response format

http_input_node_configuration.critical_note: Query parameters are ONLY accessible via InputLocalEnvironment.HTTP.Input.QueryString when parseQueryString="true" is configured on the HTTP Input node. Without this setting, query parameters will not be parsed and will be NULL.

mistake.problem: Calling CopyMessageHeaders() before accessing query parameters
mistake.symptom: Query parameters not accessible or lost
mistake.solution: Do not call CopyMessageHeaders() at the start of validation logic. Access query parameters directly from InputLocalEnvironment first, then build OutputRoot as needed.
mistake.example_wrong:
CREATE FUNCTION Main() RETURNS BOOLEAN BEGIN CALL CopyMessageHeaders(); -- WRONG: This may clear important data
DECLARE location CHARACTER; SET location = InputLocalEnvironment.HTTP.Input.QueryString.location; -- May not work after copy ... END;

mistake.problem: Using wrong path for query parameters or missing parseQueryString configuration
mistake.solution: 1. Ensure HTTP Input node has parseQueryString="true" configured in the message flow 2. Use InputLocalEnvironment.HTTP.Input.QueryString.parameterName to access parameters

common_causes.cause: HTTP Input node missing parseQueryString="true" attribute
Using InputRoot.HTTPInputHeader path instead of InputLocalEnvironment path

mistake.problem: Not handling missing or empty parameters
mistake.symptom: Unexpected NULL values causing errors downstream
mistake.solution: Always validate query parameters and provide appropriate error responses

guideline.name: Verify error responses
guideline.description: Ensure proper HTTP status codes and error messages for invalid input

expected_responses.response: 400 Bad Request for missing required parameters
400 Bad Request for invalid parameter values

best_practices_summary.practice: Configure parseQueryString="true" on HTTP Input node in message flow
Validate all required query parameters before processing
Return appropriate HTTP status codes (400 for bad requests, 200 for success)
Provide clear error messages indicating which parameters are missing or invalid
Store validated parameters in Environment.Variables for downstream node access
Provide default values for optional parameters

example_curl_commands.description: Example curl commands for testing query parameter handling
